text: Thank you very much to the committee for the opportunity to present. My name is Benjamin Flink, and I'm going to be presenting a case from Emory University of a robotic excision of a Eureka remnant with an associated infected cyst. We have no industry relationships to disclose. Patient is a seventy-four-year-old male who presented with abdominal pain, a right-paramedian abdominal mass that was erythematous. He had fevers, chills, and weakness. A urinary examination revealed a UTI, and blood cultures showed bacteremia. His past medical history was only notable for hypertension. He had no prior abdominal surgeries.

The video displays the external appearance of an abscess and a CT scan showing it as completely extraperitoneal. The CT scan highlights the top of the bladder with three arrows, and the intra-abdominal portion corresponding to the urachal cyst is marked between two arrows. A single arrow indicates calcification between the abscess and the urachal cyst inside the abdomen.

Approximately one third of all adults on autopsy studies will have some sort of microscopic remnant, but the frequency of uracal remnants that become clinically apparent is very rare, and the exact incidence is unknown. A very rare condition with a poor prognosis that must always be considered with urechal remnants is urechal carcinoma. The most common presentation in approximately fifty percent of cases is hematuria, which is followed by lower urinary tract symptoms, mucinuria, and least commonly, a mass. The risk of urechal carcinoma is higher in patients who present over the age of fifty-five and have a calcification. These patients specifically require a careful workup and examination for cancer in both the remnant as well as in the bladder.

As a first step, an incision and drainage was first performed to allow the cavity to drain, and the patient was discharged home with a Foley and a week of oral antibiotics with a plan to excise the uracral cyst in the future, similar to the treatment plan of Tazi et al. in their case series of uracral remnants. Due to the patient's age at presentation, calcification, and concern for malignancy, a cystoscopy was performed prior to the excision on the day of the operation, which showed no signs of a mass or obvious connection.

In the operating room, the surgical team performs a side dock on the patient's right side. A 12mm camera port is placed in the left mid-abdomen, with two 8mm robotic ports on either side and a 5mm assistant port near Palmer's point, triangulating on the abscess cavity. The bladder is filled with methylene blue to help identify any urinary tract infection or bladder injury. The surgeon dissects the urachal cyst away from the anterior abdominal wall, carefully separating the peritoneum and fatty tissues surrounding the median umbilical ligament without violating the fascia.

The surgeon encounters calcification during dissection and sets it aside for removal later. Dissection proceeds along the peritoneum between the anterior abdominal wall and the median umbilical ligament, revealing a soft tissue plane between the urachal cyst and the bladder. The urachal cyst is carefully dissected free from the bladder, achieving hemostasis and avoiding bladder injury. The median umbilical ligament is isolated above the bladder and dissected free before being ligated using bipolar cautery and scissors.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the urachal cyst by removing the remaining tissue connecting it to the abdominal wall. The peritoneum is then closed with a single layer of interrupted vicryl sutures.

The surgeon places the excised urachal remnant and calcification into a retrieval bag and closes the port sites. A cystogram is performed postoperatively to confirm bladder integrity, followed by the removal of the Foley catheter.
